revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:白山権現]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Hakusan Myori Daigongen.png|venerated_in={{ill|Hakusan cult|qid=Q11579557|1=Hakusan Faith}}|equivalent5_type=Japanese |equivalent5={{ill|Kikurihime|qid=Q11619891|1=Kikurihime}}}}
[[File:Hakusan Myori Daigongen.png|thumb|150px|Hakusan Gongen (Butsuzō Zui)]] '''Hakusan Gongen''' (白山権現) refers to a syncretic deity of {{ill|Mount Haku|en|Mount Haku|qid=Q2639098|lt=Hakusan|1=Mount Haku}}, integrating {{ill|Mountain worship|en|Mountain worship|qid=Q93697439|lt=Mountain worship|1=Mountain worship}} and [[Shugendō]] within the framework of [[shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Shinto and Buddhism). It is identified with {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon Bosatsu|1=Ekādaśamukha}} as its honji-butsu (original Buddhist form). It was also known as '''Hakusan Daigongen''' and '''Hakusan Myōri Gongen'''. Before the [[Shinbutsu bunri|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism]] (shinbutsu bunri) and the [[Haibutsu Kishaku|anti-Buddhist movement]] (haibutsu kishaku), Hakusan Gongen was venerated at Hakusan Gongen shrines across Japan.
== Overview == In {{ill|717|en|717|qid=Q23532|lt=717|1=717}} (first year of {{ill|Yōrō|en|Yōrō|qid=Q1373061|lt=Yōrō|1=Yōrō}}), the ascetic monk {{ill|Taichō|en|Taichō|qid=Q2388439|lt=Taichō|1=Taichō}} climbed Mount Hakusan, the main peak of {{ill|Kaga Province|en|Kaga Province|qid=Q907469|lt=Kaga Province|1=Kaga Province}} (then part of {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|qid=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province|1=Echizen Province}}) and meditated on Gozen-ga-Mine. During his meditation, the deity {{ill|Kuzuryū|en|Kuzuryū|qid=Q13577962|lt=Nine-Headed Dragon King|1=Kuzuryū}} (Kuzuryūō), a manifestation of the Eleven-Headed Kannon, emerged from the green waters of Suigatani Pond, declaring itself an incarnation of {{ill|Izanami|en|Izanami|qid=Q682306|lt=Izanami|1=Izanami}} under the name '''Hakusan Myōjin''' or '''Myōri Daibosatsu'''. This event marked the origin of Hakusan worship and the establishment of Hakusan Shugendō.<ref name="denki">"Kaga Hakusan Denki no Koto."</ref><ref name="oshou">"Taichō Oshō Denki."</ref><ref>"Hakusan, Tateyama, and Hokuriku Shugendō," ed. Shigeo Takase, ISBN 978-4626015945.</ref><ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://shofu.pref.ishikawa.jp/shofu/shirayama/japanese/data/inscription/page01.html|title=Hakusan no Ki (Modern Edition)|publisher=Ishikawa Prefectural Information Office|access-date=2009-10-29}}</ref>
The three peaks of Hakusan were associated with different manifestations: * '''Gozen-ga-Mine''': Hakusan Myōri Gongen, linked to {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Ekādaśamukha|1=Ekādaśamukha}}. * '''Bessan''': Daigyōji Gongen, a manifestation of {{ill|Āryāvalokiteśvara|ja|聖観音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred Kannon|lt=Shōkannon|qq=}}, acting as an assistant to Izanami's divine functions.<ref name="denki"/><ref name="oshou"/> * '''Ōnanji-ga-Mine''': Ōnamuchi no Mikoto (Ōnaji Gongen), appearing in the form of an old man, also assisting Izanami.<ref name="denki"/><ref name="oshou"/>
These three deities were collectively referred to as the '''Three Gongen of Hakusan'''. Additionally, as Hakusan Shugendō flourished, the five Ōji Gongen, considered retinue deities of Hakusan Myōri Gongen, were also venerated.
{| class="wikitable" ! Deity !! Alternate Name !! Honji-Butsu |- |rowspan="3"|Three Gongen |Hakusan Myōri Gongen || {{ill|Ekādaśamukha|en|Ekādaśamukha|qid=Q11404731|lt=Eleven-Headed Kannon|1=Ekādaśamukha}} |- |Daigyōji Gongen (manifestation of [[Kikurihime]]) || {{ill|Āryāvalokiteśvara|ja|聖観音|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sacred Kannon|lt=Shōkannon|qq=}} |- |Ōnamuchi Gongen (Ōnamuchi no Mikoto) || [[:en:Amida Nyorai|Amida Nyorai]] |- |rowspan="5"|Five Ōji Gongen |Tarō Ōji || [[Acala]]<ref name="dairoku">[[Shintōshū]], Volume 6, "Hakusan Gongen Story."</ref> |- |Jirō Ōji || [[:en:Kokūzō Bosatsu|Kokūzō Bosatsu]]<ref name="dairoku"/> |- |Saburō Ōji || [[:en:Jizō Bosatsu|Jizō Bosatsu]]<ref name="dairoku"/> |- |Shirō Ōji (also [[:en:Bishamonten|Bishamonten]]) || {{ill|Manjushri|en|Manjushri|qid=Q471696|lt=Monju Bosatsu|1=Manjushri}}<ref name="dairoku"/> |- |Gorō Ōji || [[:en:Miroku Bosatsu|Miroku Bosatsu]]<ref name="dairoku"/> |}
In {{ill|782|en|782|qid=Q30550|lt=782|1=782}} (first year of {{ill|Enryaku|en|Enryaku|qid=Q1373176|lt=Enryaku|1=Enryaku}}), Hakusan Gongen is said to have appeared at the foot of Mount Hachioji.<ref>"Hakusan Hime Jinja Ryakushi."</ref> In {{ill|858|en|858|qid=Q30371|lt=858|1=858}} (second year of {{ill|Ten'an|en|Ten'an|qid=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}}), it was enshrined at {{ill|Mount Hiei|en|Mount Hiei|qid=Q748040|lt=Mount Hiei|1=Mount Hiei}}'s [[:en:Enryakuji|Enryakuji]], becoming one of the guest shrines of the [[Sannō Shrine|Sannō Seven Shrines]].
=== Syncretism and Separation === The main shrine at Hakusan's summit and Hakusanji's Hakusan Hongū functioned within a honji-suijaku framework: in Shinto, Hakusan Gongen was Izanami, and the deity was identified as '''Hakusan Hime no Kami'''.<ref name="kagabamba">"Hakusan Faith and the Kaga Base," co-authored by Yamagishi.</ref> However, over time, textual confusion led to alternative interpretations identifying [[Kikurihime]] as Hakusan Hime no Kami.<ref name="kagabamba"/>
With the [[Meiji Restoration]] came the forced separation of Shinto and Buddhism. Many Shugendō sites were dismantled: * '''Kaga Province''': Hakusan Hongū was abolished and replaced by [[Hakusan Hime Shrine]]. * '''Echizen Province''': The temple Ryōzan Heisenji was replaced by [[Heisenji Hakusan Shrine]]. * '''Mino Province''': Chūgū Nagataki-ji was split into [[Nagataki Hakusan Shrine]] and [[Nagataki Temple]].
Today, most Hakusan Gongen shrines have become [[Shinto]] Hakusan Shrines dedicated to Kikurihime.
== Temples Dedicated to Hakusan Gongen == Despite the changes, a few temples continue to venerate Hakusan Gongen: * [[Natadera]] (Ishikawa Prefecture) * Upper Daigo [[:en:Daigoji|Daigoji]] (Kyoto Prefecture) * [[Engyōji]] (Hyōgo Prefecture) * [[Daihonzan Eiheiji]] (Fukui Prefecture)
Additionally, monks from [[:en:Eiheiji|Eiheiji]] make an annual pilgrimage to Hakusan's summit to conduct a ritual service.
<gallery widths="200px" heights="150px"> Shrine of EnMusubiHakusanDaigongen.jpg|Enmusubi Hakusan Daigongen (Daigoji, Upper Daigo) Hiraizumi Hakusan Myori-do 202211.jpg|Hakusan Myōri-dō<ref>{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=http://www.shirayama.or.jp/hakusan/yashiro/y162.html|title=Hakusan Shrine Tour (162) Hakusan Myōri-dō (Hakusan Shrine) (Iwate Prefecture)|access-date=2022-12-29|publisher=Hakusan Hime Shrine}}</ref> (Hiraizumi, Iwate Prefecture) </gallery>
== Mantra == The mantra for the honji-butsu Eleven-Headed Kannon is sometimes recited: * '''Oṃ mahā kāruṇika svāhā''' (Japanese transliteration: オン マカ キャロニキャ ソワカ)
== Trivia == The phrase "Atago Hakusan" used in {{ill|Jōruri|en|Jōruri|qid=Q3274260|lt=Jōruri|1=Jōruri}} and {{ill|Bunraku|en|Bunraku|qid=Q243170|lt=Bunraku|1=Bunraku}} refers to [[Atago Gongen]] and Hakusan Gongen.
== Notes == {{Reflist|2}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Hakusan|en|Hakusan|qid=Q4494945|lt=Hakusan|1=Hakusan}} - One of Japan's three sacred mountains. * {{ill|Hakusan Faith|ja|白山信仰}} - The mountain worship centered on Hakusan. * [[Hakusan Shrines]] - List of Hakusan shrines.
== External Links == * [http://shofu.pref.ishikawa.jp/shofu/shirayama/japanese/data/inscription/page01.html Hakusan no Ki (Modern Edition)] Ishikawa Prefectural Information Office
{{Shinto Horizontal}}
